topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter sat on the kitchen counter, crisp and mocking. Leo traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the cheap veneer of the counter cool beneath his touch. He didn't look up when his roommate, Mark, started recounting the triumphant stories of the team that had just snagged him. Leo just stared at the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunbeam slicing across the linoleum floor. Every word Mark spoke about their synergy, their shared drive, felt like a physical weight pressing down on Leo’s chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. He picked at a loose thread on his worn hoodie, the fabric rough against his skin, wishing the floor would just swallow him whole.

[story 2]

"Well, that's that, then," Coach Miller had said, his voice booming with forced enthusiasm. Leo stood by the locker room door, the metal cold against his back, absorbing the pronouncement like a sponge soaking up water. He didn't offer a response. Instead, he pulled his worn duffel bag tighter, the familiar weight a small, grounding anchor in the sudden, vast emptiness of disappointment. His jaw was tight, clenched so hard his molars ached. He could feel the heat rising in his neck, a flush that had nothing to do with exertion and everything to do with the bitter taste coating the back of his throat. He just wanted to walk out, leave the echoing clang of the locker room behind.

[story 3]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window of the gym hallway. The face staring back looked hollowed out, the usual spark extinguished. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless, jerky movement that betrayed the stillness inside. Everyone else was laughing, high-fiving, their energy a vibrant, undeniable current I couldn't seem to tap into. I watched them from the periphery, my shoulders slumped forward, a deliberate attempt to minimize my presence. When the coach finally approached, offering a handshake, my grip was surprisingly limp, a dead weight he could easily shake off. I mumbled something indistinct, my gaze fixed firmly on the scuff marks on the polished floor, refusing to meet anyone’s eyes.

He didn't look up when his roommate, Mark, started recounting the triumphant stories of the team that had just snagged him.
